data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Harness can strictly beat SC even at very low base error e (outside the fragile zone) provided the critic has near-perfect precision and the question pool contains at least one item where SC fails due to a bare-majority error cluster; the critical variable is not average e but the tail probability of a wrong-majority event times the critic's ability to catch it without collateral damage.", "mechanism": "SC fails only on items where >= ceil(k/2) candidates are wrong. Even at e=0.05 with k=5, the probability of 3+ errors in a batch is C(5,3)*0.05^3*0.95^2 + ... ~= 1.2%. Over 100+ items this produces measurable SC failures. If the critic has P=1.0 (zero false flags) and R>0, then every flagged item is a true error, so filtering can only help: it removes errors from broken majorities (potentially fixing them) and never damages correct majorities. The net gain is strictly positive whenever there exists at least one broken-majority item AND the critic flags at least one error candidate in that batch. The observed v4-flash data (P=0.333) does not refute this because P was far below 1.0; the false flags erased the gains. The v4-pro hard data (e=0.01) had at most 1 broken-majority item in 20 questions, yielding no measurable gap.", "scope": {"covers": ["P=1.0 (perfect precision), any R>0, any e>0, large question pool (n>=100)", "regimes where SC ceiling is not truly reached because rare broken majorities exist", "counterexample: P < 1.0 at low e where false flags erase the rare gains"], "excludes": ["P < 1.0 with small n (false-flag noise dominates)", "e so low that no broken-majority event occurs in the sample", "critic with access to answer key (trivial P=1, R=1)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "At k=5, e=0.05, P=1.0, R=0.5, n=100: harness does NOT strictly beat SC (no gain despite perfect precision and at least one broken-majority item)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "At k=5, e=0.05, P=0.95, R=0.5, n=100: harness strictly beats SC by the same margin as P=1.0 (false flags at P=0.95 cause zero damage, contradicting the claim that P<1.0 erodes gains)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "At k=5, e=0.05, P=1.0, R=1.0, n=100: SC and harness tie at 1.00 (no broken-majority event occurs, contradicting the predicted 1.2% event rate)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This is the atypical candidate: it claims the fragile-zone boundary is not a necessary condition because rare tail events at low e can still produce SC failures that a perfect-precision critic can fix. The decisive experiment requires a large pool (n>=100) to observe the rare events and a critic architecture that achieves near-perfect precision (e.g., a critic that only flags when it assigns >0.95 probability of error). Budget: 100 items * (5 candidates + 5 critiques + 5 re-votes) * ~500 tokens = ~250k tokens, within budget. The counterexample-first stance is honored by the P=0.95 forbidden case: if false flags at 5% cause zero damage, the P=1.0 sufficiency claim is weakened."}
